Ready to Go Beyond

Different objective lenses (e.g., 10X, 40X) to achieve
better magnification and resolution under visible
light. Explore different parts of a microscope
(Fig. 2.2) in your school laboratory and
materials. Under the microscope, you
will see a magnified image of an object. Can you
estimate its actual size?

Activity 2.1: Let us estimate the size of a cell
1. Take a transparent ruler with millimetre (mm)
markings.
2. Place the ruler on the stage of microscope, focus
on it using the adjustment knob and observe the
diameter of the circular field of view through
the eyepiece and measure it in mm.
3. Convert the diameter from mm to micrometre
(µm). Suppose the diameter of the visible field
is 5 mm, meaning 5 × 1000 = 5000 µm.
4. Remove the ruler and place an onion peel slide
on the stage of the microscope.
5. Focus on the slide and count the number of cells
present along the diameter of the field of view in
one straight line.
6. Estimate the real size of the cell using the
formula:

Diameter of the visible field in micrometre
Number of cells along the diameter

Estimate size of the onion peel cell = 

Unit conversion:
1 millimetre (mm) = 1000 micrometre (µm)

Fig. 2.3: An electron microscope

Apart from light microscopes,
scientists also use powerful electron
microscopes that reveal the fine details
of a cell structure. These instruments
use a beam of electrons instead of light
to produce highly magnified images,
allowing us to see structures at
the nanometre scale with remarkable
clarity (a nanometre is one-billionth of
a metre).

Thus, a microscope allows us to see very small structures clearly and is an
essential tool for studying cell structure. Over the years, scientists have
improved the microscope by improving its three main features — resolution
(measure of clarity), contrast (the difference in brightness between various

diagrams.label: Structure of a light microscope
diagrams.description: A detailed, labeled diagram of a light microscope. The diagram shows the base at the bottom, supporting the body tube. Attached to the base is the stage, which holds the slide. Below the stage are the objective lenses, which are positioned closest to the sample. The body tube connects the objective lenses to the eyepiece, which is located at the top. Controls for focusing are visible: a coarse adjustment knob and a fine adjustment knob are positioned on the side of the microscope body.

diagrams.label: An electron microscope
diagrams.description: A detailed, labeled diagram of an electron microscope. The structure is complex, showing various components necessary for generating and manipulating an electron beam. The diagram illustrates the path of the electron beam through several lenses and detectors, indicating the source of the beam and the final viewing area.
labels: Electron microscope components (various lenses and detectors)
